documents.source: Matías Romero Avendaño, finance minister in the government of President Porfirio Díaz, letter to Mexican factory workers who had gone on strike, 1892
documents.content:
The government is not indifferent to the evils afflicting the working class of the Republic: if their wages are
inadequate or if they lack employment, the first one to regret this situation is the president. However, these
are private ills that fall largely beyond the government’s power to correct. Such is the case, unfortunately, of
the problems that afflict the working class that you so honorably represent.
Given the laws that govern our country, the government cannot restrict the freedom of factory owners to fire
or hire workers, nor can it intervene directly in the improvement of basic working conditions. No laws permit
this nor do any economic interests oblige the government to dictate salaries, or prices, or working hours.
In your demands, you invoke the right to work. But this right also implies the obligation to find jobs. It
cannot be the government’s responsibility to supply workers with jobs, or to compel anyone else to supply
them. Labor is subject, by unavoidable natural phenomena, to the law of supply and demand.

documents.source: José Guadalupe Posada, Mexican printmaker famous for his depictions of political events, illustration for a popular news print describing the government’s suppression of street protests in Mexico City after a disputed election in which Porfirio Díaz was reelected president, despite allegations of widespread voter fraud, 1892
documents.content:
The Elisha Whittelsey Collection, The Elisha Whittelsey Fund, 1946
Translation of newspaper title and subtitle above the image:
STREET GAZETTE. This newspaper is published when sensational events demand it.

documents.source: José Francisco Godoy, Mexican consul in New York City, toast at a banquet given by the New York Chamber of Commerce in celebration of the good business relationship between Mexico and the United States,
documents.content:
The Waldorf Astoria Hotel, New York City, 1908.
Gentlemen, if we compare the Mexico of today with the Mexico of thirty years ago, the justice and the
administrative abilities of our President Porfirio Díaz become apparent (applause). In the Mexico of today
peace and tranquility reign supreme through the land, public education has made great progress, sanitary
regulations are strictly adhered to, railways have been built, and public safety and the rights of foreigners (as
well as of Mexicans) are rigidly safeguarded. The [United States] Department of Commerce and Labor
recently praised the leaders of the Mexican Republic who, “realizing the necessity for outside aid in
developing the natural resources of their country, have wisely framed such laws as are required to guarantee
the commercial, financial, and industrial interests of [American] businesses and have thereby achieved the
constant increase of foreign capital invested in Mexico.”
All that I have said is sufficient to demonstrate the flourishing condition of Mexico and that we Mexicans, by
placing General Díaz at the head of the administration of our country have, as you say, “the right man, in the
right place, at the right time.” (applause)

documents.source: John Kenneth Turner, United States journalist who had lived in Mexico during the last years of the Porfirio Díaz regime, Barbarous Mexico, travelogue published in Chicago, 1910. Slavery in Mexico! Yes, I found it. I found it first in Yucatan
documents.content:
The climate of northern Yucatan happens to be
perfectly adapted to the production of henequen.* Here we find vast henequen plantations extending for
miles and miles. The owners of these great plantations are the chief slave-holders of Yucatan and the
plantation workers are the slaves. The slaves are 8,000 Yaqui Indians imported from Northern Mexico, 3,000
Chinese and Koreans, and between 100,000 and 125,000 native Mayas, who formerly owned the lands that
the henequen planters now own.
The planters do not call their workers “slaves” especially when speaking to strangers. They do not call their
system slavery; they call it enforced service for debt. At first I thought it would not be so bad if the servant
had an opportunity of buying back his freedom. But I found that such was not the custom. “You need have no
fear in purchasing this plantation,” one owner said to me, “of the laborers being able to buy their freedom and
leave you. They can never do that.” “It is very easy to recruit workers,” another planter told me. “All that is
necessary is that you get some free laborer in debt to you, and then you have him. We are always getting new
laborers in that way.”
in agricultural harvesting and baling machines

documents.source: Marion Letcher, United States consul in Chihuahua, Northern Mexico, “United States, British, French, and Mexican Investment in Mexico,” report commissioned by the United States Senate subcommittee on Mexico, 1911
documents.content: See table image — Foreign investment in Mexico by type and nationality, in millions of dollars.

documents.source: Ricardo Flores Magón, Mexican intellectual and social reform activist of Native American ethnicity, “The Right of Property,” editorial published in the revolutionary newspaper Regeneración (Rebirth), March, 1911
documents.content:
The right of property is an absurd right because it had its origins in crime, fraud, and abuse of power. In the
past, land was worked in common, forests provided firewood to the hearths of all, harvests were distributed
among the members of the community according to their needs. In Mexico, this custom thrived in indigenous
communities in the era of Spanish domination and existed until recently. The attempt to take away the
common lands of indigenous communities caused the recent Yaqui Wars in Sonora and the troubles with the
Mayas in the Yucatan.
The return of the land to the peasants should be accomplished during the present uprising. We revolutionaries
will not be committing a crime by turning over the land to the working people, because, by natural law, it
already belongs to them. It is the land that their ancestors watered with their sweat; the land that the
Spaniards robbed by force from our Indian fathers. That land belongs to all Mexicans. Some of those who
own the land currently might have bought it; others might have acquired the land by taking advantage of their
friendship with corrupt government officials. Others still acquired the land by giving loans with high interest
to the small indigenous farmers, forcing them to leave the land because they could not repay the debt.

documents.source: Anonymous author, “The Death of Emiliano Zapata,” a corrido* folk song lyrics, 1919. Listen, dear sirs, to the corrido relating a sad event; For Zapata, the great insurgent has been killed
documents.content:
The good Emiliano who loved the poor and wanted to give them freedom;
for this the Indians of the villages joined him in his fight.
Zapata was born among the poor, lived among the poor, and fought for the poor.
“I don’t want riches, I don’t want honors,” he said to all.
Mister Zapata, terror of the gachupines** has died!
Where is our leader Zapata who was the punishment of the rich?
He said to his followers: “When I am dead, fight to defend your communal lands, like a man should!”
He said to his loyal assistant: “As long as I am alive, the Indians will be the owners of their land.”
Little stream, what did the flowers tell you?
“They said that Zapata is not dead, and that he will return.”
printed in pamphlet form and either sold or distributed for free by politicians or interest groups.
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt: Evaluate the extent to which economic factors led to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution (1910–1920).
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis must provide a historically defensible thesis or claim that establishes a position on the extent to which economic factors led to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution (1910–1920). The thesis or claim must either provide some indication of the reason for making that claim OR establish categories of the argument. Must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion.

sample_responses.response: Mexico's inability to resist the political dominance of the United States and European powers was the most significant factor in leading to the revolution because foreign dominance prevented the Mexican government from enacting economic reforms.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of a thesis that earns the point — establishes a clear line of reasoning that evaluates the topic by identifying a specific cause (foreign dominance) and explaining why.

sample_responses.response: Although noneconomic factors such as government corruption and ethnic tensions contributed to the Mexican Revolution, economic factors such as labor exploitation and economic inequality were the most important factors in sparking the revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (noneconomic vs. economic factors) that evaluates the extent of economic causation.

sample_responses.response: Economic factors were critical in leading to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of a response that does NOT earn the point — provides a historically defensible claim but does not establish a line of reasoning or indicate why/how extent is determined.

sample_responses.response: The Mexican Revolution is important to understand because it is the southern neighbor of the United States.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — does not focus on the topic of the prompt (economic factors and the outbreak of the revolution).

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.

required_elements: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution (1910–1920). CB-listed acceptable contexts include: neocolonialism in Latin America; the spread of liberal economic ideas; the spread of industrialization; the development of new social classes. The context must be more than a phrase or reference — appropriate elaboration required.

sample_responses.response: Like many former Spanish colonies, after independence Mexico suffered from overreliance on foreign investment and capital, which hurt Mexico's ability to direct its own economic development.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable contextualization — describes Mexico's post-independence structural dependency and explicitly ties it to the prompt's economic causation argument.

sample_responses.response: The 1910s were a period of conflict, because World War I was going on at the same time as the Mexican Revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — does not provide context relevant to the topic of the prompt (economic factors in the Revolution's outbreak).

sample_responses.response: Neocolonialism hurt many economies in Latin America.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — provides only a passing phrase or reference without developed elaboration connecting neocolonialism to the specific prompt.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses the content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt. The response must accurately describe — rather than simply quote — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of economic factors and their contribution to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution (1910–1920).

sample_responses.response: (Document 1): The finance minister tells strikers that unemployment is the result of supply and demand and is out of the government's hands.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of describing the content of a document relevant to the topic — accurately paraphrases Doc 1's laissez-faire response to striking workers.

sample_responses.response: The documents mention many problems.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague reference to 'the documents' without describing content from three specific documents — fails the CB standard of accurately describing content.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs+: Support an argument using at least four documents.

required_elements: Support an argument using at least four documents.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Support an argument using at least four documents.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using at least six documents (CB 2021 standard, combined evidence row). The response must accurately use the content of at least six documents to support an argument in response to the prompt. The six documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: (Document 1): The finance minister tells strikers that unemployment is the result of supply and demand and is out of the government's hands, a position which probably increased people's discontent with the government because they were unwilling to help. (Connects the contents of the document to an argument about the extent to which economic factors contributed to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution.)
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of supporting an argument using a document's content — explicitly connects Doc 1 to an argument about economic discontent with the Díaz regime.

sample_responses.response: (Document 2): The newspaper cartoon shows that the government was willing to use violence to put down popular protests against a rigged election system. Such oppressive government policies may have contributed to increased support for the eventual revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — uses Doc 2 (Posada cartoon) to support an argument about how political oppression compounded economic grievances.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 describes the government. Document 2 shows a protest. Document 3 is about trade.
sample_responses.commentary: Lists documents without using them to support a specific argument — does not explicitly connect content to an argument about economic causation.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.

required_elements: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence (beyond that found in the documents) relevant to an argument about the extent to which economic factors led to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution (1910–1920). CB-listed acceptable outside evidence includes: specific instances of European economic imperialism in Asia, Africa, and Latin America; socialist revolts in Europe (Paris Commune, Bolshevik Revolution, revolt in Germany at end of WWI); revolutions in South America and Mexico against Spanish rule during the nineteenth century; abolition of slavery in specific Latin American states (e.g., Cuba, Brazil); specific US military interventions in Latin America (e.g., Spanish-American War); Pancho Villa expedition in 1916. Must be different from contextualization and more than a phrase.

sample_responses.response: Economic problems caused by industrialization and economic inequality led to revolts around the world, not just in Mexico, but also in Russia with the Bolshevik Revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of evidence beyond the documents — names a specific event (Bolshevik Revolution) and connects it to an argument about economic causes of revolution.

sample_responses.response: Although slavery was abolished throughout Latin America over the 1800s, new ways of exploiting labor were developed, including indentured labor. This meant that working conditions in Mexico remained very bad, leading to a lot of unhappiness with the Mexican government.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — identifies specific outside evidence (abolition of slavery in Latin America + rise of indentured labor) relevant to an argument about economic grievances driving the revolution.

sample_responses.response: There were also other important factors in this period.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague and nonspecific — does not name a specific piece of evidence beyond the documents.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.

required_elements: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For at least three documents (CB 2021 standard), explains how or why the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, and/or audience is relevant to an argument. The response must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the POV, purpose, situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of the three documents sourced.

sample_responses.response: (Document 3): As a diplomatic representative of the Diaz government, Godoy is focused only on the positive aspects of United States investment in Mexico and lavishes praise on Diaz. (Identifies the point of view of the source and how this might be relevant to the extent to which economic factors contributed to the outbreak of the Mexican Revolution.)
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable POV analysis — explains how Godoy's diplomatic position shapes his selective emphasis, relevant to evaluating economic causation.

sample_responses.response: (Document 7): The corrido is meant to memorialize a hero of the revolution and to motivate the people to carry on his struggle, and to 'punish the rich' and make 'Indians owners of their lands,' both of which were major economic goals of the revolutionaries.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable purpose analysis — explains how the Zapata corrido's mobilizing purpose is relevant to an argument about the economic goals of the revolution.

sample_responses.response: (Document 6): Ricardo Magón uses the history of Native Americans, especially the taking of their land and their oppression by white colonizers to argue for a revolution that would take back the land and give it to the workers, pointing out the fundamental corruption that allowed Spanish elites to acquire the land that the native Americans now work.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example of acceptable historical situation analysis — explains how Magón's indigenous framing shapes the economic argument of the document.

sample_responses.response: Document 7 shows how popular Zapata was among the common people and that many people in Mexico were sad that he was killed.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — identifies the content of the document but does NOT explain how/why POV, purpose, situation, or audience is relevant to an argument.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.

required_elements: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. CB-listed ways to demonstrate complexity include: analyzing multiple variables (e.g., how economic exploitation coincided with ethnic minority oppression, using Docs 6 and 7 to show revolutionary appeals to Native dispossession alongside Doc 4's Yaqui Indian enslavement); explaining relevant and insightful connections across time and space (e.g., comparing motivating factors with other revolutions such as Russia prior to the Bolshevik Revolution); corroborating multiple perspectives (e.g., using Doc 3's praise of Díaz to corroborate documents showing worker and peasant problems). Must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: A response arguing that economic exploitation in Mexico often coincided with the oppression of ethnic minorities could use Documents 6 and 7 to show how some revolutionaries specifically appealed to Native histories of dispossession and complement this analysis with the description of the enslavement of Yaqui Indians in Document 4 in service of plantations producing materials for European and U.S. industry.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed pathway to complexity — analyzing multiple variables (economic + ethnic) by weaving Docs 4, 6, and 7 into a sustained argument about overlapping oppressions.

sample_responses.response: A response could draw explicit and insightful comparisons by bringing in outside information on the situation in Russia prior to the Bolshevik Revolution and then using the documents describing the situation of the peasants and workers in Mexico to show how economic problems fueled revolutionary movements in both countries.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed pathway to complexity — explaining relevant and insightful connections across time and space by comparing Mexican and Russian revolutionary causation.

sample_responses.response: In conclusion, the Mexican Revolution was complex and had many causes.
sample_responses.commentary: Single closing sentence — does not demonstrate sustained complexity through analysis of multiple variables, insightful connections, or corroboration as the CB rubric requires.
